column5: REQUIREMENT
column7: USE CASE
column8: COMMENTS/QUESTIONS

column5: What
column6: Why

column5: Provide a selected prospect for analysis
column6: The agent can perform focused pain point research on the correct company
column7: Input must at minimum include company name. Optional fields: website, country/region, division/business unit, industry and specific research lens.
column8: MVP is single-prospect focused, not batch-first. The briefing explicitly scopes the analysis to one company/prospect.

column5: Specify the content operations lens for the analysis
column6: The agent focuses on FFD-relevant pains instead of generic company research
column7: Default lens: marketing operations and Content Value Chain, especially (global-to-local) content creation, adaptation/localisation, workflow, governance, Marketing Operating Model, MarTech Health & Maturity, AI readiness, compliance, distribution, publishing and optimisation.
column8: Should be configurable only within approved scope. Avoid drifting into generic business, financial or brand analysis.

column5: Prioritise a specific division, region or market when relevant
column6: The output can be tailored to the most commercially relevant part of the prospect
column7: Example: prioritise Consumer Beauty or EMEA if public sources indicate relevance, while noting whether findings generalise across the company.
column8: If no division/region is specified, default to company-wide with global + regional lens.

column5: Prevent the agent from drifting outside the defined topic
column6: The output remains consistent, commercially relevant and consultant-ready
column7: Agent must focus on CVC, global-to-local operating model, governance, tooling/process, content workflows and pain points.
column8: Do not produce generic SWOT, financial analysis or broad company profile unless directly relevant to content operations.

column5: Execute a defined research plan before writing
column6: The analysis is systematic rather than opportunistic
column7: Agent researches marketing transformation, content workflow, governance, global rollout, embedded teams, data ownership, brand integrity and AI in marketing before drafting.
column8: This mirrors the prompt requirement to search first, then write.

column5: Use only credible and relevant sources
column6: Findings are defensible in commercial conversations
column7: Preferred sources: company press releases, investor materials, annual reports, earnings calls, reputable industry press, case studies by known firms and role descriptions/job posts.
column8: Avoid low-quality blogs, unsupported commentary and speculative sources.

column5: Support each major finding with source evidence
column6: Users can validate the analysis and reuse it with confidence
column7: Each relevant pain point and technology signal must include evidence quote or summary plus source link.
column8: If evidence is weak, mark confidence as Low and label any inference explicitly.

column5: Confirm major pain points with multiple sources where possible
column6: The strongest claims are not based on a single weak signal
column7: For major pain points, agent should try to confirm with at least two independent sources.
column8: If triangulation is not possible, state this in confidence or unknowns.

column5: Access all underlying sources used in the output
column6: The deliverable can be reviewed quickly by FFD or client-facing teams
column7: Create a source list with source title, date if available, publisher and URL.
column8: Source links should also appear close to relevant evidence in the pain point inventory.

column5: Clearly distinguish evidence from inference
column6: The analysis remains transparent and avoids overclaiming
column7: When root cause or workflow details are inferred from public evidence, label them as inference and keep them minimal.
column8: Important for externally researched prospects where internal workflows are not fully visible.

column5: Prioritise recent sources and transformation signals
column6: The output reflects the prospect’s current situation
column7: Prefer sources from the last 24 to 36 months for transformation, tooling, operating model and AI-related claims.
column8: Older sources may be used for context, but should not dominate current pain point conclusions.

column5: Detect relevant roles and hiring signals
column6: Operating model and capability gaps become more visible
column7: Identify leadership roles, job posts or team structures related to marketing ops, digital, content, creative, commerce, DAM, AI or analytics.
column8: Role signals are supporting evidence and should not alone drive high-confidence conclusions.

column5: Organise findings by the six CVC steps
column6: The output maps directly to FFD’s Content Value Chain language
column7: Use six sections: Briefing, Asset Creation, Asset Management, Intelligent Content Assembly / Modularisation, Omnichannel Publishing, Optimisation / Performance Learning.
column8: This is a core requirement from the prompt.

column3: Pain point detection per CVC step
column5: Identify global-to-local pain points for each CVC step
column6: The agent reveals where content operations friction occurs
column7: For each CVC step, list concrete pain points related to creation, adaptation, localisation, approval, distribution or optimisation.
column8: If a CVC step has little evidence, state no strong public evidence found rather than inventing a pain point.

column5: Phrase pain points as failure of a job to be done
column6: Pain points become sharper and more actionable
column7: Example pattern: Local markets cannot efficiently adapt global hero assets into channel-ready variants without rework.
column8: Avoid generic pain point labels such as slow process or poor tooling.

column5: Link each pain point to likely root causes
column6: FFD can translate findings into solution hypotheses
column7: Root causes must be categorised as process, governance, tooling, capability and/or operating model.
column8: If root cause is inferred, label it as likely or inferred.

column5: Identify who is impacted by each pain point
column6: FFD can understand stakeholder relevance and buying committee implications
column7: Include impacted groups such as global marketing, regional teams, local markets, agencies, digital commerce, creative operations and brand governance.
column8: Use public role evidence where available; otherwise use generic stakeholder groups.

column5: Rate severity of each pain point
column6: Users can prioritise the most material issues
column7: Use High, Medium or Low severity based on business impact, frequency/scale signals and executive relevance.
column8: Severity should not be presented as exact measurement unless supported by sources.

column5: Rate confidence for each pain point or major claim
column6: Users can distinguish strong evidence from weak evidence
column7: Use High, Medium or Low confidence. Confidence should reflect source quality, recency and triangulation.
column8: Confidence is mandatory for the pain point inventory.

column5: Map who does what from global to regional to local
column6: The operating model and handoffs become visible
column7: Produce a text-based workflow map covering global brief, master asset creation, regional adaptation, local execution, approvals, publishing and feedback.
column8: Should include agencies where evidence or logical workflow role indicates agency involvement.

column5: Identify key handoffs and approval moments
column6: FFD can spot workflow friction and governance bottlenecks
column7: Highlight likely handoffs between global, regional, local, agencies, legal/compliance and brand governance.
column8: If exact approvals are unknown, mark as inference and include in unknowns.

column5: Assess whether the model appears centralised, federated or decentralised
column6: The analysis can inform FFD’s operating model conversation
column7: Use evidence from organisation design, regional empowerment, embedded teams, governance and tooling investments.
column8: Use cautious language if public evidence is limited.

column5: Extract recurring patterns of localisation friction
column6: The output reveals where FFD can create value
column7: Look for signals around approvals, localisation, templates, modularity, asset reuse, rights/compliance, channel variants, agency model and measurement loops.
column8: These patterns should feed into the pain point inventory.

column5: Receive a concise executive summary
column6: Sales and strategy teams can quickly understand the opportunity
column7: Executive summary must contain 8 to 12 bullets covering top global-to-local pain points and why they matter.
column8: Crisp, non-fluffy, consultant-ready tone.

column5: Receive a structured pain point inventory
column6: Findings can be reviewed, filtered and translated into action
column7: Required columns: CVC step, pain point, who is impacted, evidence, likely root causes, severity and confidence.
column8: This is the central deliverable of the agent.

column5: Identify tooling signals and their relevance
column6: Potential Adobe, MarTech and Content Supply Chain opportunities become visible
column7: Include mentions of DAM, workflow tools, GenAI content platforms, approval tooling, PIM, CMS, analytics or performance tooling.
column8: Explain how each signal relates to global-to-local friction.

column5: Receive intervention opportunities
column6: The output becomes commercially actionable for FFD
column7: Opportunities may include process, operating model, governance, modular content, templates, automation/AI and performance learning improvements.
column8: Each opportunity must map back to one or more pain points.

column3: What we still do not know section
column5: See key information gaps and next best sources
column6: Discovery conversations can be prepared more intelligently
column7: Include unknowns around bottlenecks, tool adoption, modularity, agency role, feedback loops, division differences and channel differences where relevant.
column8: Also propose next best stakeholder interviews or source types to close gaps.

column5: Receive a complete source appendix
column6: The deliverable is auditable and reusable
column7: List all sources used, including URL, source type and date where available.
column8: Must be included in final deliverable document.

column5: Receive concise, sharp and non-fluffy output
column6: The deliverable can be used directly in FFD business development workflows
column7: Use clear headings, bullet points, tables and explicit confidence labels. Avoid long generic prose.
column8: Tone should match the Coty example deliverable.

column5: Receive the final output as a structured report document
column6: The deliverable can be shared internally and prepared for prospect conversations
column7: Generate a final report similar to the Coty pain point research output, with executive summary, workflow map, CVC pain points, tooling signals, unknowns, opportunities and source list.
column8: Implementation format to be confirmed by AI Lab: Word document, structured Markdown, or app-generated report.

column5: Map each opportunity to specific pain points
column6: Recommendations are grounded and not generic
column7: Every recommended intervention should reference related CVC step(s), pain point(s) and root cause(s).
column8: This prevents generic FFD capability pitching.

column5: Highlight where FFD capabilities may be relevant
column6: The analysis can support ABM and sales preparation
column7: Map pain areas to themes such as Content Value Chain, Content Operating Model, modular content, content supply chain, DAM, workflow and AI-enabled content operations.
column8: Keep as opportunity hypotheses, not a hard sales pitch.

column5: Identify Adobe-related signals if present
column6: Adobe partnership or solution fit can be assessed
column7: Detect references to Adobe Experience Cloud, AEM, DAM, Workfront, GenStudio, Firefly, Analytics, Campaign, AJO, RT-CDP or related roles/projects.
column8: Only include when evidence exists; no assumption based on company size.

column5: Surface signals that may indicate urgency
column6: FFD can prioritise timely outreach or discovery
column7: Detect transformation programs, AI initiatives, cost reduction, reorganisation, regional model changes, performance pressure or executive statements.
column8: Must be evidence-backed and tied to possible CVC pain. Avoid self-fulfilling prophecies.

column5: Identify likely stakeholder groups for discovery
column6: Sales preparation becomes more targeted
column7: Suggest likely stakeholders such as CMO, VP Marketing, Global Brand Lead, Content Operations, Creative Operations, Digital Commerce, Marketing Technology and regional/local marketing leads.
column8: Must be framed as likely stakeholder groups unless named individuals are sourced.

column5: Prevent unsupported conclusions
column6: The output remains credible and client-safe
column7: Agent should not state internal process details as fact unless directly supported by evidence.
column8: Use phrases such as public evidence suggests when required.

column5: Handle limited public evidence gracefully
column6: The agent still produces useful output without inventing details
column7: If evidence is limited, produce a shorter report, lower confidence ratings and a clear unknowns section.
column8: Do not force all CVC steps to have high-severity pain points.

column5: Remove irrelevant or duplicate findings
column6: The final report stays concise and useful
column7: Merge similar pain points, remove generic company facts and exclude sources that do not support CVC/global-to-local analysis.
column8: Important for consultant-ready quality.

column5: Track when and how the report was generated
column6: Outputs remain auditable over time
column7: Include prospect name, date of run, agent version, prompt version and research scope in the final output.
column8: Useful when reports are refreshed over time.
